title: Severe Anorexia Nervosa (Restricting Type) in a 16-Year-Old Girl Admitted for Medical Stabilization at 71% of Expected Body Weight with Bradycardia (HR 38 Asleep), Hypotension, Hypothermia, Orthostasis, and QTc Prolongation: Preventing and Managing REFEEDING SYNDROME (Hypophosphatemia + Hypokalemia + Hypomagnesemia + Thiamine Depletion) with Cautious 'Start-Low-Go-Slow' Nutritional Rehabilitation, Electrolyte Repletion, Continuous Cardiac Monitoring, Structured Supervised Meals, and Family-Based Multidisciplinary Treatment
subtitle: ANOREXIA NERVOSA (AN) = a psychiatric eating disorder (DSM-5) with the HIGHEST mortality of any psychiatric illness (from medical complications + suicide) defined by: (1) restriction of energy intake relative to requirements leading to SIGNIFICANTLY LOW BODY WEIGHT for age/sex/development/health; (2) intense FEAR of gaining weight or persistent behavior interfering with weight gain despite low weight; (3) DISTURBANCE in the way body weight/shape is experienced, undue influence of weight on self-evaluation, or denial of the seriousness of low weight; SUBTYPES = RESTRICTING (weight loss via dieting/fasting/excessive exercise) vs BINGE-EATING/PURGING; severity by BMI in adults; in adolescents use % of expected body weight + growth curves (this patient 71% of expected body weight = severe). AN is NOT a lifestyle choice or vanity — it is a serious mental illness, frequently comorbid with anxiety, depression, OCD, and trauma, with high suicide risk. MEDICAL COMPLICATIONS OF STARVATION (multisystem; drive medical admission): CARDIAC — sinus BRADYCARDIA (adaptive hypometabolism; HR may be <40, especially asleep), HYPOTENSION, ORTHOSTASIS, reduced cardiac mass, pericardial effusion, prolonged QTc + arrhythmia risk, mitral valve prolapse; sudden cardiac death; HYPOTHERMIA; ELECTROLYTE abnormalities (worse with purging — hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis); ENDOCRINE — amenorrhea, low estrogen/testosterone, euthyroid-sick, hypoglycemia, growth/pubertal delay; BONE — osteopenia/osteoporosis; HEME — leukopenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia; GI — delayed gastric emptying, constipation, elevated transaminases; DERM — lanugo (fine body hair), dry skin, hair loss, carotenoderma, Russell's sign (knuckle calluses from self-induced vomiting) + dental erosion/parotid swelling if purging; RENAL; NEURO — pseudoatrophy. CRITERIA FOR MEDICAL HOSPITALIZATION (AAP/SAHM) include: HR <50 daytime / <45 at night (bradycardia), systolic BP <90, orthostatic changes (HR rise >20 or BP drop >10-20), hypothermia <36°C, <75% expected body weight (or acute rapid weight loss), electrolyte disturbance, ECG abnormality (prolonged QTc/arrhythmia), syncope, acute food refusal, uncontrolled bingeing/purging, hematemesis, or comorbid conditions requiring stabilization. THE CENTRAL INPATIENT DANGER = REFEEDING SYNDROME: when nutrition is reintroduced after prolonged starvation, the carbohydrate load triggers an INSULIN SURGE that drives PHOSPHATE, POTASSIUM, and MAGNESIUM intracellularly (and increases cellular uptake for anabolism), causing potentially fatal HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA (the HALLMARK — phosphate is needed for ATP + 2,3-DPG + cardiac/respiratory muscle), HYPOKALEMIA, and HYPOMAGNESEMIA; concurrent THIAMINE (B1) depletion (consumed in carbohydrate metabolism → Wernicke risk); and a shift to sodium/water RETENTION causing fluid overload + edema → manifesting as cardiac ARRHYTHMIA + heart failure, respiratory failure, rhabdomyolysis, seizures, delirium, and death, typically within the first days of refeeding. PREVENTION/MANAGEMENT OF REFEEDING SYNDROME: (1) identify high-risk patients (very low weight, minimal intake, prior low electrolytes, alcohol/purging); (2) CHECK baseline phosphate, potassium, magnesium, and glucose and CORRECT deficiencies BEFORE/while starting feeds; (3) give THIAMINE BEFORE or with the start of refeeding (and before any IV dextrose) to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy; (4) 'START LOW, GO SLOW' — begin at a conservative calorie level and advance gradually per protocol while monitoring (note: contemporary protocols favor somewhat higher starting calories with vigilant electrolyte monitoring + repletion rather than excessive caution that prolongs malnutrition — but always with close monitoring); (5) MONITOR electrolytes frequently (e.g., q12h initially) and REPLETE phosphate/potassium/magnesium proactively; (6) CONTINUOUS CARDIAC MONITORING + daily weights + strict intake/output + watch for edema/fluid overload; (7) prefer ORAL/enteral nutrition; avoid rapid IV dextrose/fluids. NURSING CARE (Basic Care & Comfort heavy): structured, SUPERVISED MEALS with a set meal plan + a calm, non-negotiating, supportive presence; OBSERVE during and for ~1 hour AFTER meals to prevent purging/hiding/discarding food + excessive exercise; standardized WEIGHTS (often 'blind'/back-to-scale, gowned, after voiding, same time/scale each morning) per protocol to reduce anxiety + ensure accuracy; monitor vitals/orthostatics/temperature + cardiac rhythm; provide warmth + comfort for hypothermia; manage constipation/early satiety/bloating; therapeutic, non-judgmental relationship that separates the person from the illness; assess SUICIDE risk; do NOT collude with the eating disorder (e.g., excessive low-cal substitutions) but also avoid power struggles. TREATMENT FRAMEWORK = medical stabilization → nutritional rehabilitation/weight restoration → PSYCHOTHERAPY: FAMILY-BASED TREATMENT (FBT/Maudsley) is the first-line, evidence-based treatment for adolescents (empowers PARENTS to take charge of refeeding/meals initially, then gradually returns control to the adolescent); CBT-E for adults; medications have a LIMITED role (no FDA-approved drug for AN; olanzapine may modestly help weight/obsessionality; SSRIs treat comorbid anxiety/depression but are ineffective for core AN at low weight); treat comorbidities; long-term outpatient multidisciplinary follow-up. DEVELOPMENTAL/ETHICAL: adolescent care is family-centered with parental involvement + the patient's assent; severe AN can impair insight/judgment, and life-threatening refusal may rarely require involuntary treatment, but the standard approach is engagement + FBT + the least restrictive effective care

patient.name: E. Lindqvist
patient.code: Full code; minor — parents provide consent + are central to treatment (Family-Based Treatment); developmentally appropriate assent sought; goals = medical stabilization + safe weight restoration + treat the eating disorder + family-supported recovery
patient.admitDate: Admitted to the pediatric/adolescent medical unit for MEDICAL STABILIZATION of severe anorexia nervosa (restricting type). Over the past 10 months she progressively restricted intake, eliminated 'fear foods,' exercised compulsively (running + hours of activity daily), and lost ~14 kg, now at 71% of expected body weight (BMI 14.2; previously tracking ~50th percentile). Parents brought her after she fainted at school. On arrival: profound BRADYCARDIA (HR 44 awake, 38 asleep on telemetry), HYPOTENSION (88/54), HYPOTHERMIA (35.4°C), ORTHOSTATIC vitals positive, and ECG with QTc 478 ms; labs notable for low-normal phosphate, low-normal potassium, low magnesium, mild transaminitis, leukopenia. Meets multiple criteria for inpatient medical admission. Plan: cardiac monitoring, cautious nutritional rehabilitation with refeeding-syndrome prevention, electrolyte repletion, thiamine, structured supervised meals, and initiation of Family-Based Treatment with psychiatry, dietitian, and social work.
patient.room: Adolescent medical unit, room with continuous cardiac telemetry; calm, supportive environment; bathroom privileges supervised initially (purging/exercise precautions); parents involved per FBT; multidisciplinary team (adolescent medicine + child/adolescent psychiatry + registered dietitian + nursing + social work + family therapist + school liaison)

vitals.temp: 35.4°C (hypothermic); thin/cachectic with lanugo on forearms + dry skin + cool extremities + delayed capillary refill; alert + oriented + quiet + minimizes concerns ('I'm fine, I just want to be healthy'); cardiac telemetry initiated; weight 41 kg (71% expected body weight; BMI 14.2); ECG QTc 478 ms; no chest pain; denies suicidal ideation on screening but flat affect + anxious about meals

vitals.hr: 38 (asleep; sinus bradycardia — physiologic adaptation to starvation; reassessed: perfusing, asymptomatic, sinus rhythm with no pauses/blocks)
vitals.temp: 35.6°C; asleep + arousable + asymptomatic; warming measures (warmed blankets) applied; bradycardia of starvation is expected and improves with nutritional rehabilitation — distinguished from a pathologic arrhythmia (sinus rhythm, no symptoms, no pauses); continuous monitoring maintained; baseline electrolytes being repleted

35.8°C; PRE-REFEEDING LABS: phosphate 2.6 mg/dL (low-normal), potassium 3.4 (low), magnesium 1.5 (low), glucose 72; THIAMINE administered before starting feeds; electrolytes being repleted (oral/IV phosphate, potassium, magnesium); nutritional rehabilitation started at a CONSERVATIVE calorie level per refeeding protocol via structured oral meals + supplement; q12h electrolyte monitoring scheduled; daily weight (gowned, post-void, back-to-scale) protocol; strict I&O; cardiac monitoring continued; dietitian + psychiatry + FBT engaged with parents

36.1°C; on day 2 of refeeding the PHOSPHATE dropped to 2.0 mg/dL (hypophosphatemia — the hallmark refeeding marker) with potassium 3.3 + magnesium 1.5 → aggressive repletion (phosphate + potassium + magnesium) + calorie advancement HELD/slowed per protocol while electrolytes corrected; no edema, no arrhythmia, no respiratory distress; supervised meals completed with support; observed 1 hr post-meal (no purging/hiding); thiamine continued; close monitoring

36.4°C; electrolytes corrected + stable (phosphate 3.4, potassium 4.0, magnesium 2.0) with continued repletion + monitoring (now daily); calories cautiously advanced per protocol without recurrence of hypophosphatemia; weight up 0.8 kg (appropriate, not excessive — watching for fluid/edema vs true gain); QTc improving (462 ms); orthostasis resolving; tolerating structured supervised meals; engaging slightly more with psychiatry; parents trained in FBT meal support

36.6°C; medically stabilizing — HR ≥50 daytime sustained, normothermic, no orthostasis, electrolytes stable on oral repletion, QTc 448 ms, steady appropriate weight gain, no refeeding complications; transitioning toward step-down/partial-hospitalization or specialized eating-disorder program with continued Family-Based Treatment + outpatient medical + psychiatric + dietitian follow-up; comorbid anxiety being addressed; parents empowered + supported; school re-entry planned

labs.v: 16 y/o girl E. Lindqvist admitted for MEDICAL STABILIZATION of severe ANOREXIA NERVOSA, RESTRICTING TYPE. HISTORY: over ~10 months progressive dietary restriction beginning after comments about her body + a focus on 'clean/healthy' eating that escalated into rigid rules, elimination of 'fear foods,' calorie counting, fluid restriction, and COMPULSIVE EXERCISE (daily running + hours of activity, exercising even when exhausted); lost ~14 kg, now at 71% OF EXPECTED BODY WEIGHT (BMI 14.2; previously tracked ~50th percentile on growth curves); secondary AMENORRHEA × 5 months; cold intolerance, fatigue, dizziness, constipation, hair thinning, difficulty concentrating; intense fear of weight gain + body-image disturbance + denies the seriousness of her low weight ('I'm fine, I just want to be healthy'). Parents brought her after she FAINTED (syncope) at school. PSYCH: high-achieving, perfectionistic, anxious temperament; comorbid generalized anxiety + features of OCD (rigidity, rituals around food); mood low; SUICIDE screen today negative but flat affect — ongoing risk assessment essential (AN carries high suicide risk). No prior eating-disorder treatment. PMH otherwise healthy. SOCIAL: lives with both parents + a younger brother; supportive but frightened, exhausted family who feel helpless; strong family unit, engaged; competitive cross-country runner + excellent student; some social withdrawal recently; no substance use. ADMISSION FINDINGS meeting multiple AAP/SAHM criteria for inpatient medical admission: BRADYCARDIA (HR 44 awake, 38 asleep on telemetry), HYPOTENSION (88/54), positive ORTHOSTATIC vitals (HR rise >20/BP drop), HYPOTHERMIA (35.4°C), <75% expected body weight (71%), ECG QTc PROLONGATION (478 ms), syncope, and electrolyte abnormalities. Exam: cachectic + lanugo + dry skin + cool extremities + delayed capillary refill + flat affect. PLAN: continuous cardiac monitoring + cautious nutritional rehabilitation with REFEEDING-SYNDROME prevention + electrolyte repletion + thiamine + structured supervised meals + initiation of FAMILY-BASED TREATMENT with multidisciplinary team.
labs.r: Severe anorexia nervosa (restricting type) at 71% expected body weight with bradycardia + hypotension + hypothermia + orthostasis + QTc prolongation + syncope + electrolyte abnormalities = meets multiple criteria for medical admission. AN has the highest mortality of any psychiatric illness. RN role: recognize medical instability + the psychiatric illness + initiate safe stabilization.

labs.n: Medical complications of starvation + cardiac risk
labs.v: MULTISYSTEM COMPLICATIONS OF STARVATION explain the admission findings and guide monitoring: CARDIAC (the main acute danger) — sinus BRADYCARDIA is a physiologic adaptation to conserve energy in starvation (HR often <50, may be <40 asleep) and, when it is sinus rhythm in an asymptomatic perfusing patient, is expected and improves with nutrition (distinguish from pathologic arrhythmia/heart block); reduced myocardial mass + HYPOTENSION + ORTHOSTASIS (volume depletion + autonomic dysfunction); prolonged QTc + increased risk of ventricular arrhythmia/sudden cardiac death (worsened by hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia → monitor + replete); pericardial effusion; mitral valve prolapse. HYPOTHERMIA (loss of insulating fat + hypometabolism) → provide warmth. ELECTROLYTES — restricting type may have relatively preserved electrolytes initially but they fall with refeeding; PURGING (not this patient) causes hypokalemia + hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis. ENDOCRINE — hypothalamic AMENORRHEA (low LH/FSH/estrogen), euthyroid-sick (low T3), hypercortisolism, HYPOGLYCEMIA (depleted glycogen/gluconeogenic substrate — can be life-threatening), growth/pubertal delay in adolescents. BONE — low estrogen + low weight + hypercortisolism → osteopenia/OSTEOPOROSIS (fracture risk; not reversed by oral contraceptives — weight restoration is key). HEMATOLOGIC — bone-marrow suppression: leukopenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia. GI — delayed gastric emptying + early satiety + bloating + CONSTIPATION + elevated transaminases (hepatic stress/autophagy); superior mesenteric artery syndrome with rapid weight loss. DERMATOLOGIC — LANUGO (fine downy hair), dry skin, hair loss, carotenoderma (yellow palms), brittle nails; if purging: Russell's sign (knuckle calluses), dental enamel erosion, parotid hypertrophy. NEURO — cerebral pseudoatrophy + cognitive slowing (reversible). RENAL — impaired concentrating ability. NURSING IMPLICATIONS: continuous cardiac monitoring + scheduled orthostatic vitals + temperature + warmth measures + glucose monitoring + watch for arrhythmia especially during refeeding electrolyte shifts; recognize that bradycardia/hypotension/hypothermia improve with weight restoration; bed rest/activity restriction initially to reduce cardiac demand + prevent compulsive exercise.
labs.r: Starvation causes multisystem complications: bradycardia/hypotension/orthostasis/QTc + hypothermia (cardiac danger), amenorrhea/hypoglycemia, osteoporosis, marrow suppression, GI slowing/transaminitis, lanugo. Sinus bradycardia of starvation is expected + improves with feeding (vs pathologic arrhythmia). RN role: cardiac + orthostatic + temp + glucose monitoring + warmth + activity restriction.

labs.v: REFEEDING SYNDROME is the central, potentially fatal inpatient danger when nutrition is reintroduced after starvation. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: during starvation the body shifts to fat/protein catabolism, depletes total-body PHOSPHATE/POTASSIUM/MAGNESIUM (even if serum levels look near-normal because they are intracellular/whole-body depleted) and THIAMINE, and down-regulates insulin. When CARBOHYDRATE is reintroduced, INSULIN SURGES → drives glucose, PHOSPHATE, POTASSIUM, and MAGNESIUM rapidly INTO cells (for glycogen/protein synthesis + phosphorylated intermediates) → serum levels plummet: HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA is the HALLMARK (phosphate is essential for ATP and 2,3-DPG → without it: cardiac + respiratory + skeletal muscle failure, hemolysis, rhabdomyolysis, seizures, delirium), HYPOKALEMIA (arrhythmia), HYPOMAGNESEMIA (arrhythmia + refractory hypokalemia/hypocalcemia); THIAMINE is consumed in carbohydrate metabolism → acute deficiency → WERNICKE encephalopathy; insulin + carbohydrate also cause renal SODIUM and WATER RETENTION → fluid overload + EDEMA + congestive heart failure (a weakened myocardium); net result = cardiac ARRHYTHMIA/arrest, heart failure, respiratory failure, neurologic events, and death, typically within the FIRST few days of refeeding. HIGH-RISK features: very low weight/BMI, little or no intake for days, significant weight loss, low baseline phosphate/potassium/magnesium, alcohol misuse, purging. PREVENTION/MANAGEMENT: (1) IDENTIFY high-risk patients (this patient qualifies); (2) CHECK baseline phosphate, potassium, magnesium, calcium, glucose and CORRECT deficiencies before/while initiating feeds; (3) administer THIAMINE before or with the start of refeeding and before any IV dextrose (prevents Wernicke); (4) 'START LOW, GO SLOW' — initiate at a conservative calorie level and advance gradually per institutional protocol (modern protocols may start somewhat higher with vigilant monitoring/repletion to avoid 'underfeeding,' but always cautious in the highest-risk and always with close electrolyte surveillance); (5) MONITOR electrolytes frequently (q12h initially, then daily) and REPLETE phosphate/potassium/magnesium PROACTIVELY — if phosphate falls, replete and hold/slow calorie advancement until corrected; (6) CONTINUOUS CARDIAC MONITORING + daily weights + strict intake/output + assess for EDEMA/fluid overload + respiratory status; (7) prefer ORAL/enteral nutrition; AVOID rapid IV dextrose or large fluid boluses; (8) supplement multivitamin/micronutrients. THIS PATIENT: thiamine given before feeds; conservative start; on day 2 phosphate dropped to 2.0 → aggressive repletion + calorie advancement held until corrected; no arrhythmia/edema; electrolytes normalized by day 5 with continued careful advancement.
labs.r: Refeeding syndrome: insulin surge after carbohydrate reintroduction drives phosphate/potassium/magnesium intracellularly → HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA (hallmark) + hypokalemia + hypomagnesemia + thiamine depletion + fluid retention → arrhythmia/heart failure/respiratory failure/seizures/death in first days. Prevent: check + replete electrolytes, THIAMINE before feeds, start low/go slow, monitor electrolytes q12h, cardiac monitoring, watch edema. RN role: vigilant monitoring + proactive repletion + cautious advancement.

labs.n: Nutritional rehabilitation + supervised meals + nursing care (Basic Care & Comfort)
labs.v: NUTRITIONAL REHABILITATION + SUPPORTIVE NURSING CARE is the core daily work and is heavily Basic-Care-&-Comfort focused: (1) STRUCTURED MEAL PLAN — a dietitian-designed plan with set meals/snacks and calorie targets that advance per protocol; meals are NON-NEGOTIABLE in content (avoid bargaining/substitutions that collude with the eating disorder) but delivered with warmth + support; (2) SUPERVISED MEALS — the nurse/staff provides calm, encouraging PRESENCE during meals, sets a time limit, offers gentle coaching, and does NOT engage in debates about calories; (3) POST-MEAL OBSERVATION — observe during and for ~1 HOUR AFTER meals/snacks to prevent compensatory behaviors (self-induced vomiting, hiding/discarding food, exercising); supervise bathroom use after meals initially; (4) ACTIVITY RESTRICTION — limit exercise (bed rest or restricted activity initially) to reduce cardiac demand and counter compulsive exercise; (5) STANDARDIZED WEIGHTS — weigh per protocol, typically MORNING, after voiding, in a gown, often 'BLIND' (patient back-to-scale, not told the number) to reduce anxiety + prevent water-loading or weight manipulation (check for hidden weights/water-loading; consider checking urine specific gravity if manipulation suspected); (6) COMFORT MEASURES — provide WARMTH for hypothermia (warmed blankets); manage CONSTIPATION + early satiety/bloating + GI discomfort (the slowed gut makes refeeding uncomfortable — reassure these are expected/temporary); skin/oral care; rest; (7) FLUIDS — monitor hydration without permitting water-loading; (8) THERAPEUTIC RELATIONSHIP — non-judgmental, consistent, externalize the illness (separate the person from the 'eating disorder voice'), validate feelings while holding the structure, avoid power struggles + avoid being punitive; reinforce that recovery is possible; (9) SAFETY — ongoing suicide-risk assessment + a safe environment; (10) DOCUMENT intake (% of meal completed), behaviors, vitals, weight, electrolytes, and emotional state. The nurse balances firm, consistent structure (the eating disorder thrives on flexibility/negotiation) with genuine compassion — this combination, plus parental involvement, is therapeutic.
labs.r: Nursing care = structured non-negotiable meal plan + calm supervised meals + ~1 hr post-meal observation (prevent purging/hiding/exercise) + activity restriction + standardized (often blind) weights + warmth/comfort for hypothermia + manage constipation/bloating + therapeutic non-judgmental relationship that externalizes the illness + suicide-risk assessment + accurate documentation. RN role: firm-but-compassionate structure + comfort + safety.

labs.v: TREATMENT FRAMEWORK for anorexia nervosa: STAGED — (1) MEDICAL STABILIZATION (correct life-threatening instability + prevent refeeding syndrome — the current admission); (2) NUTRITIONAL REHABILITATION + WEIGHT RESTORATION (essential — many cognitive/mood symptoms partially reverse with weight restoration; you cannot effectively do the psychological work at very low weight); (3) PSYCHOTHERAPY: FAMILY-BASED TREATMENT (FBT / Maudsley approach) is the FIRST-LINE, evidence-based treatment for ADOLESCENTS with AN — it is non-blaming, agnostic about cause, and EMPOWERS PARENTS to take temporary charge of re-nourishing their child (planning + plating + supervising meals) in Phase 1, then gradually returns age-appropriate control over eating to the adolescent (Phase 2) and addresses normal adolescent development (Phase 3); for adults, enhanced CBT (CBT-E) is first-line; (4) treat COMORBIDITIES (anxiety, depression, OCD). MEDICATIONS have a LIMITED role: there is NO FDA-approved medication for AN; OLANZAPINE (atypical antipsychotic) may modestly aid weight gain + reduce obsessional thinking in some; SSRIs do NOT treat core AN at low weight (and are less effective when malnourished) but may help comorbid anxiety/depression after some weight restoration; avoid bupropion (seizure risk in eating disorders); monitor QTc with any QT-prolonging agent. LEVELS OF CARE: inpatient medical → inpatient psychiatric/specialized eating-disorder unit → residential → partial hospitalization (PHP) → intensive outpatient (IOP) → outpatient, stepped to acuity. DEVELOPMENTAL/ETHICAL: adolescent care is FAMILY-CENTERED with parents as consenting decision-makers + the adolescent's developmentally appropriate ASSENT and voice; confidentiality is balanced with safety + parental involvement; severe AN can impair insight/judgment about the medical danger, and in rare life-threatening cases involuntary treatment/nutrition may be ethically/legally invoked under the least-restrictive principle — but the standard approach is engagement, FBT, and building motivation rather than coercion; involve school + peers in re-entry; address the family's exhaustion + guilt (it is NOT their fault) + equip them. PROGNOSIS: variable; early intervention + weight restoration + FBT improve outcomes; relapse is common → long-term multidisciplinary follow-up.
labs.r: Treatment = medical stabilization → weight restoration → psychotherapy; FAMILY-BASED TREATMENT (FBT) is first-line for adolescents (parents lead refeeding, then return control); CBT-E for adults; meds limited (no FDA-approved drug; olanzapine may help; SSRIs only for comorbidities + ineffective for core AN at low weight). Family-centered + assent + least-restrictive care. RN role: support staged treatment + FBT + family + comorbidity + follow-up.

labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES: the bradycardia is SINUS bradycardia of starvation (telemetry shows sinus rhythm, no pauses/heart block/ventricular ectopy, asymptomatic + perfusing) — NOT a primary pathologic arrhythmia (though QTc prolongation + electrolyte shifts mean arrhythmia risk must be monitored); NO current arrhythmia or cardiac arrest; NO signs of refeeding-induced heart failure/edema/respiratory distress to date (prevented by cautious protocol + repletion); NO purging behaviors (restricting type — so no hypochloremic alkalosis/Russell's sign/dental erosion, though electrolytes still fall with refeeding); NO hematemesis/GI bleed; NO superior mesenteric artery syndrome symptoms; NO Wernicke encephalopathy (thiamine given before feeds); NO hypoglycemic crisis (glucose monitored); NO active suicidal ideation on current screening (but ongoing risk assessment essential given AN's high suicide risk + comorbid mood/anxiety); NO drug/alcohol use; NKDA. NEGATIVES distinguishing care: this is NOT a case for rapid IV dextrose/aggressive fluids (would precipitate refeeding syndrome); NOT a case for unsupervised meals/bathroom or unrestricted exercise; NOT a case for negotiating meal content with the eating disorder. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: caught and admitted before cardiac arrest/death; ENGAGED, supportive, intact family ready for Family-Based Treatment (the single strongest predictor of adolescent recovery); ADOLESCENT (shorter illness duration + FBT highly effective + better prognosis than long-standing adult AN); early in illness course (~10 months); thiamine + electrolyte monitoring + cautious refeeding PREVENTED severe refeeding syndrome (day-2 hypophosphatemia caught + corrected without complication); multidisciplinary eating-disorder-experienced team; good baseline health + intelligence + motivation to be 'healthy' (a foothold for engagement); school + social supports; no purging/substance use; comorbid anxiety identified for treatment. EXPECTED trajectory: medical stabilization (HR ≥50 daytime, normothermia, no orthostasis, stable electrolytes, improving QTc, steady weight gain) → step-down to specialized eating-disorder program/PHP with continued FBT + outpatient medical/psychiatric/dietitian follow-up + school re-entry; recovery is achievable, especially with early FBT, though vigilance for relapse is needed long-term.
labs.r: Key negatives: sinus bradycardia of starvation (not pathologic arrhythmia), no refeeding heart failure/edema yet, no purging, thiamine given (no Wernicke), no active SI (but keep assessing). Protective: caught before arrest + engaged family for FBT + adolescent/early illness + cautious refeeding prevented complications + experienced team. Recovery achievable with staged treatment + FBT + long-term follow-up.

nursesNote: Admission 1600 — 16 y/o girl E. Lindqvist admitted to adolescent medical unit for MEDICAL STABILIZATION of severe ANOREXIA NERVOSA, RESTRICTING TYPE. HISTORY: ~10 months of progressive restriction (began after body comments + 'clean eating' → rigid rules + fear foods eliminated + calorie/fluid restriction + COMPULSIVE EXERCISE), lost ~14 kg, now 71% of expected body weight (BMI 14.2; previously ~50th %ile); secondary amenorrhea × 5 mo; cold intolerance + fatigue + dizziness + constipation + hair thinning + poor concentration; intense fear of weight gain + body-image disturbance + minimizes seriousness ('I'm fine, I just want to be healthy'); brought in after SYNCOPE at school. PSYCH: perfectionistic + anxious + features of OCD around food + low mood; suicide screen negative today but flat affect — ongoing risk assessment essential (AN = highest mortality psych illness). SOCIAL: lives with both parents + younger brother; supportive but frightened/exhausted family, engaged; competitive cross-country runner + strong student; recent social withdrawal; no substance use. ADMISSION (meets multiple AAP/SAHM medical-admission criteria): HR 44 awake / 38 asleep (sinus BRADYCARDIA), BP 88/54 with positive ORTHOSTATICS, T 35.4°C (HYPOTHERMIA), 71% expected body weight, ECG QTc 478 ms, syncope, electrolyte abnormalities; exam cachectic + lanugo + dry skin + cool extremities + delayed cap refill + flat affect. Continuous cardiac telemetry initiated; activity restriction; warmed blankets. NIGHT 0300 — telemetry low alarm HR 38 asleep: assessed → SINUS rhythm, no pauses/block, asymptomatic, perfusing = expected bradycardia of starvation (distinguished from pathologic arrhythmia); warming continued; monitoring maintained. DAY 1 0700 — PRE-REFEEDING labs: phosphate 2.6 (low-normal), K 3.4 (low), Mg 1.5 (low), glucose 72; THIAMINE given BEFORE starting feeds (Wernicke prevention); electrolytes repleted (phosphate/K/Mg); nutritional rehabilitation started at CONSERVATIVE calories per refeeding protocol via structured oral meals + supplement; q12h electrolyte monitoring; daily weight protocol (morning, post-void, gowned, BLIND/back-to-scale); strict I&O; cardiac monitoring continued; dietitian + child/adolescent psychiatry + social work + Family-Based Treatment initiated with parents. SUPERVISED MEALS with calm supportive presence + time limits + no negotiating meal content; observed 1 hr POST-meal (no purging/hiding/exercise); supervised bathroom initially. DAY 2 1800 — REFEEDING-SYNDROME WATCH: phosphate dropped to 2.0 (HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA — hallmark) with K 3.3 + Mg 1.5 → aggressive repletion + calorie advancement HELD/slowed per protocol until corrected; NO edema/arrhythmia/respiratory distress; meals completed with support; thiamine continued; close monitoring. DAY 5 0700 — electrolytes corrected/stable (phosphate 3.4, K 4.0, Mg 2.0) with continued repletion + monitoring (now daily); calories cautiously advanced without recurrent hypophosphatemia; weight up 0.8 kg (appropriate — watching fluid/edema vs true gain); QTc improving (462); orthostasis resolving; tolerating supervised meals; engaging more with psychiatry; parents trained in FBT meal support. DAY 10 — MEDICAL STABILIZATION: HR ≥50 daytime sustained (58), normothermic (36.6), no orthostasis, electrolytes stable on oral repletion, QTc 448, steady appropriate weight gain, no refeeding complications; transitioning to specialized eating-disorder program/PHP with continued FBT + outpatient medical + psychiatric + dietitian follow-up; comorbid anxiety addressed; parents empowered + supported; school re-entry planned. Throughout: firm-but-compassionate structure, externalize the illness, non-judgmental therapeutic relationship, comfort measures (warmth, constipation/bloating management), ongoing suicide-risk assessment, accurate documentation of meal % + behaviors + vitals + weight + electrolytes + affect.
providerOrders: ADMIT to adolescent medical unit with continuous cardiac telemetry

DIAGNOSIS: severe anorexia nervosa (restricting type), 71% expected body weight, with bradycardia + hypotension + hypothermia + orthostasis + QTc prolongation + syncope; refeeding-syndrome risk; comorbid anxiety
CODE STATUS: full code; minor — parents consent + central to Family-Based Treatment; adolescent assent
MONITORING:
Continuous cardiac telemetry (bradycardia + QTc + refeeding arrhythmia risk); document rhythm; notify provider for non-sinus arrhythmia, pauses/block, symptomatic bradycardia, or QTc lengthening
Vital signs with ORTHOSTATICS + temperature q4h (and per protocol); warming measures for hypothermia
Daily morning WEIGHT — post-void, gowned, BLIND (back-to-scale) per protocol; assess for water-loading/hidden weights (urine specific gravity if manipulation suspected)
Strict intake & output; document % of each meal/snack completed; assess for edema/fluid overload + respiratory status
Glucose monitoring; continuous reassessment of suicide risk + mental status
Activity restriction (bed rest/limited activity) to reduce cardiac demand + counter compulsive exercise
REFEEDING-SYNDROME PREVENTION/MANAGEMENT:
Baseline + q12h (then daily) phosphate, potassium, magnesium, calcium, glucose
THIAMINE before/with initiation of feeds (and before any IV dextrose); daily multivitamin + micronutrients
Begin nutritional rehabilitation at conservative calorie level per refeeding protocol; advance gradually
PROACTIVELY replete phosphate / potassium / magnesium; if phosphate falls (e.g., <2.5-3.0) replete and HOLD/slow calorie advancement until corrected
Prefer oral/enteral nutrition; AVOID rapid IV dextrose or large fluid boluses; nasogastric feeds only if oral intake inadequate per team
NUTRITION + MEAL STRUCTURE (dietitian):
Structured meal plan with set meals/snacks + calorie targets advancing per protocol; meal content non-negotiable
Supervised meals with calm supportive presence + time limit; no debating calories/substitutions
Observe during and ~1 hour after meals/snacks (prevent purging, hiding/discarding food, exercise); supervised bathroom after meals initially
Manage constipation, early satiety, bloating (expected with refeeding); comfort/warmth measures
PSYCHIATRIC + THERAPEUTIC:
Child/adolescent psychiatry consult; treat comorbid anxiety; ongoing suicide-risk assessment + safe environment
Initiate FAMILY-BASED TREATMENT (FBT) — empower + coach parents in meal support; family therapist
Medications: no FDA-approved drug for AN; consider olanzapine per psychiatry; SSRI only for comorbidities after some weight restoration; avoid bupropion; monitor QTc with any QT-prolonging agent
Therapeutic, non-judgmental, consistent approach; externalize the illness; avoid power struggles + collusion
MULTIDISCIPLINARY:
Adolescent medicine + child/adolescent psychiatry + registered dietitian + nursing + social work + family therapist + physical/occupational therapy as appropriate + school liaison
ECG on admission + monitor QTc; echocardiogram if indicated; DEXA for bone density (osteoporosis) as outpatient
FAMILY-CENTERED + DEVELOPMENTAL + ETHICAL:
Parents as consenting decision-makers + central to FBT; adolescent assent + voice; balance confidentiality with safety
Support the family (reduce guilt/blame — not their fault; address exhaustion; equip them)
Least-restrictive effective care; involuntary nutrition only if life-threatening refusal per ethics/legal process
PATIENT + FAMILY EDUCATION + TEACH-BACK:
Medical seriousness of AN (highest-mortality psychiatric illness) + that bradycardia/hypothermia/labs improve with weight restoration
Refeeding syndrome rationale for cautious advancement + electrolyte monitoring/repletion + why structure/supervision are therapeutic (not punitive)
FBT roles for parents; meal support skills; managing the 'eating disorder voice'
Warning signs + when to seek care; relapse is common → importance of long-term follow-up
DISCHARGE/STEP-DOWN PLANNING (when medically stable — HR ≥50 daytime, normothermic, no orthostasis, stable electrolytes, steady weight gain):
Transition to specialized eating-disorder program / inpatient psych / residential / partial hospitalization per acuity, with continued FBT
Outpatient adolescent medicine + psychiatry + dietitian + therapist follow-up; school re-entry plan; crisis resources

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at this anorexia nervosa admission?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: Severe malnutrition at 71% of expected body weight (BMI 14.2) with anorexia nervosa, restricting type — a serious psychiatric illness with the highest mortality of any psychiatric disorder, not a lifestyle choice
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — severity of malnutrition + the seriousness of the illness frame the medical risk. RN role: recognize medical instability + the psychiatric illness.

opts.text: Medical instability meeting admission criteria — bradycardia (HR 44 awake / 38 asleep), hypotension (88/54), positive orthostatics, hypothermia (35.4°C), QTc prolongation (478 ms), and syncope — requiring continuous cardiac monitoring
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — cardiovascular instability is the acute danger requiring telemetry + monitoring. RN role: cardiac + orthostatic + temperature monitoring.

opts.text: REFEEDING SYNDROME risk — reintroducing nutrition can cause hypophosphatemia (hallmark) + hypokalemia + hypomagnesemia + thiamine depletion + fluid shifts → arrhythmia/heart failure/death in the first days; requires baseline + frequent electrolytes, thiamine before feeds, cautious advancement, repletion
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the central inpatient danger. RN role: vigilant electrolyte monitoring + thiamine + proactive repletion + cautious refeeding.

opts.text: Structured, SUPERVISED meals with observation during and ~1 hour after meals to prevent compensatory behaviors (purging, hiding/discarding food, compulsive exercise) + activity restriction to reduce cardiac demand
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — supervised meals + post-meal observation + activity restriction are core therapeutic nursing care. RN role: meal supervision + behavioral monitoring.

opts.text: Standardized weights (morning, post-void, gowned, often blind/back-to-scale) + assess for water-loading or hidden weights to ensure accuracy and reduce anxiety
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — accurate, anxiety-minimizing weight protocol guides treatment. RN role: standardized weighing + detect manipulation.

opts.text: Ongoing SUICIDE-RISK assessment + comorbid anxiety/depression/OCD — AN carries high suicide risk; flat affect + anxiety about meals noted despite a negative screen today
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — psychiatric safety must be continuously assessed. RN role: suicide-risk assessment + safe environment + psychiatry.

opts.text: Family-Based Treatment + a therapeutic, non-judgmental relationship that externalizes the illness — engaged parents are the strongest predictor of adolescent recovery
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — FBT + therapeutic alliance are central to recovery. RN role: support FBT + compassionate consistent structure + family engagement.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation. Relevant for medication/repletion administration. Not the priority cue.

opts.text: The sinus bradycardia (HR 38 asleep) is an immediate indication for atropine and a transcutaneous pacer regardless of rhythm or symptoms
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Sinus bradycardia of starvation in an asymptomatic, perfusing patient (sinus rhythm, no pauses/block) is an expected adaptation that improves with nutritional rehabilitation — not an indication for atropine/pacing. Monitor; treat symptomatic pathologic arrhythmia, not adaptive sinus bradycardia.

opts.text: Begin aggressive high-calorie refeeding immediately with an IV dextrose infusion and large fluid boluses to reverse the malnutrition as fast as possible
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. Aggressive refeeding + IV dextrose/fluids precipitates REFEEDING SYNDROME (hypophosphatemia, arrhythmia, fluid overload, death) and Wernicke's. Use cautious advancement, oral/enteral preference, thiamine first, and electrolyte monitoring/repletion.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: Anorexia nervosa = restriction → significantly low body weight + intense fear of weight gain + body-image disturbance/denial of seriousness; a serious psychiatric illness (highest psychiatric mortality) with multisystem starvation complications
opts.rat: CORRECT — AN definition + seriousness. Foundation for recognizing medical + psychiatric risk.

opts.text: Starvation causes sinus bradycardia/hypotension/orthostasis/QTc + hypothermia + amenorrhea + hypoglycemia + osteoporosis + marrow suppression + GI slowing/transaminitis + lanugo; sinus bradycardia of starvation improves with weight restoration (vs pathologic arrhythmia)
opts.rat: CORRECT — starvation complications + bradycardia interpretation. Foundation for monitoring.

opts.text: Refeeding syndrome — carbohydrate reintroduction → insulin surge → phosphate/potassium/magnesium shift intracellularly (hypophosphatemia hallmark) + thiamine depletion + sodium/water retention → arrhythmia/heart failure/respiratory failure/seizures; prevent with electrolyte checks + repletion + thiamine before feeds + cautious advancement + monitoring
opts.rat: CORRECT — refeeding syndrome pathophysiology + prevention. Foundation for safe nutritional rehabilitation.

opts.text: Nutritional rehabilitation requires structured non-negotiable meals + supervised eating + ~1 hr post-meal observation (prevent purging/hiding/exercise) + activity restriction + standardized blind weights — firm structure plus compassion, not collusion or power struggles
opts.rat: CORRECT — supervised meal/behavioral framework. Foundation for therapeutic nursing care.

opts.text: Treatment is staged (medical stabilization → weight restoration → psychotherapy); FAMILY-BASED TREATMENT is first-line for adolescents; medications have a limited role (no FDA-approved drug; SSRIs ineffective for core AN at low weight; treat comorbidities)
opts.rat: CORRECT — treatment framework + FBT + medication role. Foundation for the care plan.

opts.text: AN carries high suicide risk + frequent comorbid anxiety/depression/OCD → ongoing suicide-risk assessment + psychiatric care are essential even when a single screen is negative
opts.rat: CORRECT — psychiatric safety principle. Foundation for ongoing risk assessment.

opts.text: Because the patient says she just wants to be 'healthy' and looks calm, her denial of the seriousness of her low weight means she has good insight and meal supervision is unnecessary
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Denial/minimization of the seriousness of low weight is a feature of AN, not evidence of insight; supervised meals + monitoring remain essential. The illness distorts her perception of risk.

opts.text: Serum electrolytes that look near-normal on admission mean the patient is not at risk for refeeding syndrome and feeds can be advanced rapidly
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Total-body phosphate/potassium/magnesium are depleted even when serum levels look near-normal; they fall sharply once feeding starts. Risk is based on weight/intake history, and cautious advancement + monitoring are still required.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: CARDIAC stability + refeeding-syndrome prevention r/t bradycardia + QTc prolongation + electrolyte shifts — continuous telemetry, baseline + frequent phosphate/potassium/magnesium, thiamine before feeds, proactive repletion, cautious calorie advancement, watch for arrhythmia/edema
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The acute life threats (arrhythmia + refeeding syndrome) are cardiac/electrolyte. RN role: monitoring + repletion + cautious refeeding.

opts.text: NUTRITIONAL REHABILITATION via structured supervised meals r/t severe malnutrition — set meal plan, calm supervised meals, post-meal observation, activity restriction, accurate intake documentation
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Re-nourishment (done safely) is the definitive treatment + reverses many complications. RN role: supervised meals + behavioral monitoring.

opts.text: PSYCHIATRIC SAFETY r/t high suicide risk + comorbid anxiety/OCD — ongoing suicide-risk assessment + safe environment + psychiatry + therapeutic relationship
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Psychiatric safety is continuous + AN has high suicide risk. RN role: risk assessment + safe environment + alliance.

opts.text: ACCURATE MONITORING r/t weights/orthostatics/temperature/I&O — standardized blind weights, detect water-loading, orthostatic vitals, warmth for hypothermia, fluid-balance + edema surveillance
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Accurate data guides safe advancement + detects deterioration. RN role: standardized monitoring + comfort.

opts.text: FAMILY-BASED TREATMENT + therapeutic alliance + family support r/t adolescent recovery — engage/coach parents, externalize the illness, reduce family guilt, plan step-down + school re-entry
opts.rat: #5 — Important throughout + for long-term recovery. FBT + family engagement drive outcomes. RN role: support FBT + family + discharge planning.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this anorexia nervosa admission.

opts.text: Continuous cardiac telemetry + scheduled orthostatic vitals + temperature monitoring + warmth measures for hypothermia
opts.rat: Cardiac instability + hypothermia require monitoring + warming. RN role: telemetry + vitals + comfort.

opts.text: Check baseline + q12h phosphate/potassium/magnesium, give thiamine before feeds, start nutrition at a conservative level, advance gradually, and replete electrolytes proactively (hold advancement if phosphate falls)
opts.rat: Core refeeding-syndrome prevention. RN role: monitoring + thiamine + repletion + cautious advancement.

opts.text: Structured supervised meals with a set meal plan + calm supportive presence + observation during and ~1 hour after meals + supervised bathroom initially + activity restriction
opts.rat: Therapeutic meal structure + behavioral monitoring. RN role: supervised meals + post-meal observation.

opts.text: Standardized morning blind weights (post-void, gowned, back-to-scale) with assessment for water-loading/hidden weights
opts.rat: Accurate, anxiety-minimizing weighing guides treatment + detects manipulation. RN role: standardized weighing.

opts.text: Ongoing suicide-risk assessment + psychiatry consult + treat comorbid anxiety + therapeutic non-judgmental relationship that externalizes the illness
opts.rat: Psychiatric safety + comorbidity treatment + alliance. RN role: risk assessment + therapeutic relationship + psychiatry.

opts.text: Initiate Family-Based Treatment, engage and coach parents in meal support, and reduce family guilt while equipping them
opts.rat: FBT is first-line for adolescents + family engagement drives recovery. RN role: support FBT + family.

opts.text: Manage constipation, early satiety, and bloating; provide comfort and reassurance that these refeeding symptoms are expected and temporary
opts.rat: Comfort + symptom management supports tolerance of refeeding. RN role: GI comfort + reassurance.

opts.text: Begin aggressive high-calorie feeding with IV dextrose and large fluid boluses to correct malnutrition rapidly
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG/DANGEROUS — precipitates refeeding syndrome + Wernicke's. Use cautious advancement, oral/enteral preference, thiamine first, electrolyte monitoring/repletion.

opts.text: Allow unsupervised meals and bathroom use and let her exercise freely, since she is calm and cooperative
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — removes the supervision/observation/activity restriction needed to prevent purging/hiding food/compulsive exercise and to reduce cardiac demand. Calm cooperation does not eliminate eating-disorder behaviors.

opts.text: Negotiate meal content with the patient and substitute low-calorie 'safe' foods whenever she is anxious, to keep the peace
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — colludes with the eating disorder and undermines re-nourishment. Meal content is non-negotiable; provide support for the anxiety without changing the plan.

opts.text: Give atropine and prepare transcutaneous pacing for the asymptomatic sinus bradycardia of starvation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — adaptive, asymptomatic sinus bradycardia of starvation is monitored, not paced/atropinized; it improves with nutrition. Treat pathologic/symptomatic arrhythmia only.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should act. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Establish continuous cardiac telemetry + obtain orthostatic vitals/temperature + draw baseline phosphate/potassium/magnesium/glucose + ECG; apply warming measures + activity restriction
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Assess + monitor the acute cardiac/electrolyte risks and stabilize the environment. RN role: monitoring + baseline labs + safety.

opts.text: Give thiamine before feeds + correct baseline electrolyte deficiencies, then begin nutrition at a conservative calorie level per refeeding protocol
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Prevent refeeding syndrome/Wernicke before/at the start of cautious feeding. RN role: thiamine + repletion + cautious initiation.

opts.text: Provide structured supervised meals with support + observe during and after meals + initiate standardized blind weights + strict I&O + watch for edema/arrhythmia
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Deliver and monitor safe re-nourishment + detect refeeding complications. RN role: supervised meals + monitoring.

opts.text: Assess suicide risk + mental status, engage psychiatry, and build a therapeutic, non-judgmental relationship that externalizes the illness
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Psychiatric safety + alliance underpin treatment. RN role: risk assessment + therapeutic relationship + psychiatry.

opts.text: Initiate Family-Based Treatment, engage/coach parents, support the family, and plan step-down + outpatient follow-up + school re-entry
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing/foundational. FBT + family + continuity drive long-term recovery. RN role: FBT support + discharge planning.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Day 1: thiamine given before feeds; baseline electrolytes repleted; nutrition started at conservative calories per protocol; telemetry + q12h electrolytes + daily blind weights + supervised meals + FBT initiated with parents
opts.rat: Correct, comprehensive safe-refeeding management initiated. Foundation for stabilization.

opts.text: Day 2: phosphate dropped to 2.0 mg/dL (hypophosphatemia) with low K + Mg → aggressive repletion + calorie advancement held until corrected; no edema/arrhythmia/respiratory distress
opts.rat: Early refeeding hypophosphatemia was DETECTED and managed correctly (replete + hold advancement) before complications — the monitoring protocol working as intended. Trajectory is safe/improving.

opts.text: Day 5: electrolytes corrected/stable (phos 3.4, K 4.0, Mg 2.0); calories cautiously advanced without recurrent hypophosphatemia; QTc improving (462); orthostasis resolving; appropriate weight gain
opts.rat: Stable electrolytes + safe advancement + improving cardiac/orthostatic status + appropriate weight gain. Foundation for medical stabilization.

opts.text: Day 10: HR ≥50 daytime sustained, normothermic, no orthostasis, stable electrolytes on oral repletion, QTc 448, steady weight gain, no refeeding complications; transitioning to specialized program with FBT
opts.rat: Medical stabilization achieved with safe step-down planning. Excellent trajectory.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — feeds were advanced aggressively with IV dextrose and no electrolyte monitoring; on day 2 the patient develops a ventricular arrhythmia, confusion, and pulmonary edema with phosphate 1.0, potassium 2.7, magnesium 1.0
opts.rat: ⚠️ Full refeeding syndrome from aggressive feeding without monitoring/repletion/thiamine. Demonstrates why cautious advancement + electrolyte surveillance + thiamine + repletion are essential. Medical emergency.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — meals were left unsupervised and bathroom use unrestricted; the patient hides/discards food, vomits after meals, and water-loads before weights, so her weight appears stable while she continues to deteriorate
opts.rat: ⚠️ Lack of supervision/observation + non-standardized weights allowed eating-disorder behaviors + falsely reassuring weights while she worsens. Demonstrates why supervised meals + post-meal observation + blind weights matter.

opts.text: Day 3 alternative — the patient becomes tearful and discloses passive suicidal thoughts and hopelessness; the nurse performs a focused suicide-risk assessment, ensures a safe environment, and notifies psychiatry urgently
opts.rat: ⚠️ Emergence of suicidal ideation (AN carries high suicide risk). Appropriate response (assess + safe environment + psychiatry) is correct; the clinical status has worsened and requires escalation.

opts.text: Day 7 — parents, coached in FBT, successfully support a full supervised meal; the patient eats with less distress and engages more in therapy; weight gain remains steady and appropriate
opts.rat: FBT engagement + improved meal tolerance + therapeutic engagement + steady weight gain. Foundation for recovery.

opts.text: Admission-day telemetry shows HR 38 asleep in sinus rhythm, asymptomatic and perfusing, unchanged from the expected bradycardia of starvation on serial checks before nutrition has taken effect
opts.rat: Asymptomatic sinus bradycardia of starvation is expected and will not change until nutritional rehabilitation progresses; serial checks before feeding effect show it unchanged (monitored, not an emergency in itself).

opts.text: Week 4 (step-down program) — sustained weight restoration progressing, electrolytes normal off IV repletion, menses-axis recovering, engaged in FBT + treating comorbid anxiety, back to school part-time with supports
opts.rat: Sustained recovery trajectory with weight restoration + FBT + comorbidity treatment + school re-entry. Standard ideal longer-term outcome (with ongoing relapse vigilance).
